stem: A sequence is given by \( a_n = \dfrac{[[a]]n^{2} + [[b]]n + [[c]]}{[[d]]n^{2} + [[e]]n} \). Find \( \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n \).
solution: Dividing numerator and denominator by \( n^{2} \) (the highest power present), every term except the leading ones vanishes, leaving the ratio of leading coefficients \( \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[d]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the coefficients of the linear (subleading) terms instead of the leading coefficients.
This inverts the ratio of leading coefficients.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This uses the constant terms instead of the leading coefficients.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

stem: A sequence is given by \( a_n = \dfrac{[[a]]n + [[b]]}{[[d]]n^{2} + [[e]]n + [[f]]} \). Find \( \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n \).
solution: The denominator grows like \( n^{2} \), which outpaces the numerator's growth of order \( n \). Dividing through by \( n^{2} \) sends every term to \( 0 \), so \( \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} a_n = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly treats numerator and denominator as having equal degree and takes a leading-coefficient ratio.
This uses the subleading coefficients as if forming a leading-coefficient ratio.
This uses the constant terms as if forming a ratio.
This reports the numerator's leading coefficient itself instead of recognizing the limit is 0.
This assumes the limit is 1, a common but unjustified default guess.
This assumes the limit is −1, a common but unjustified default guess.

stem: A sequence is given by \( a_n = \sqrt{n^{2} + [[a]]n + [[b]]} - n \). Find \( \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n \).
solution: Multiply by the conjugate: \( \sqrt{n^{2}+[[a]]n+[[b]]} - n = \dfrac{[[a]]n + [[b]]}{\sqrt{n^{2}+[[a]]n+[[b]]} + n} \). Dividing numerator and denominator by \( n \) gives \( \dfrac{[[a]]}{1+1} = \dfrac{[[a]]}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by 2 (the two \( n \) terms in the denominator).
This wrongly assumes the radical cancels \( n \) exactly.
This uses the constant term \( b \) instead of the linear coefficient \( a \).
This mixes the linear and constant coefficients.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

stem: A sequence is given by \( a_n = [[c]] + \dfrac{[[p]]}{n^{[[k]]}} \). Find \( \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n \).
solution: As \( n \to \infty \), the term \( \dfrac{[[p]]}{n^{[[k]]}} \to 0 \), so \( \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} a_n = [[c]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly keeps the vanishing term at its \( n=1 \) value instead of letting it tend to \( 0 \).
This reports the vanishing term's coefficient instead of the constant \( c \) that the sequence actually converges to.
This wrongly assumes the entire expression (including the constant) vanishes.
This subtracts the vanishing term's coefficient instead of dropping it.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

stem: A sequence is given by \( a_n = n \sin\!\left(\dfrac{[[a]]}{n}\right) \). Find \( \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n \).
solution: Let \( t = [[a]]/n \to 0 \) as \( n \to \infty \). Then \( a_n = \dfrac{[[a]]\sin t}{t} \to [[a]]\cdot 1 = [[answer]] \), using \( \lim_{t\to 0}\frac{\sin t}{t}=1 \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes the bounded sine factor forces the whole product to \( 0 \), ignoring that \( n \) grows exactly as fast as \( \sin(a/n) \) shrinks.
This wrongly assumes the limit is the generic special-limit value \( 1 \) itself, forgetting the factor of \( a \).
This halves \( a \) instead of matching it exactly.
This squares \( a \) instead of leaving it linear.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
